sources.title: Resistor Colour Code and Resistor Tolerances Explained
Diode Voltage Drop: Values, Formula, and How to Measure It
NPN Transistor Tutorial — The Bipolar Junction NPN Transistor
1947: Invention of the Point-Contact Transistor — Computer History Museum

Superheterodyne AM Receiver — Working with Block Diagram and Schematics

structure.rationale: Start with the electrical quantities every later page reuses (voltage, current, Ohm's law) so the passive components — resistor, capacitor, inductor — can each be explained by what they do to V and I. Then cross into semiconductors via the diode (the simplest one-way junction), the triode (the historical first amplifier, for contrast), and the transistor (the modern amplifier/switch that replaced it). Close with How a Radio Works, which is the payoff: it reuses the tuned LC circuit, the diode detector, and transistor amplification from every prior page in one working system.
structure.audience: A curious beginner with no electronics background who wants a concise, accurate, example-first tour of the core components and how they combine into a real device (a radio).

claims.text: Ohm's law states V = I × R: the voltage across a resistor equals the current through it times its resistance.
Current is the same at every point of a single (series) loop; voltage divides across components in series and current divides across branches in parallel.

pitfalls: Beginners often confuse voltage (the push, like water pressure) with current (the flow, like water flow rate) — they are related by R, not interchangeable.

claims.text: A 4-band resistor's first two bands are significant digits, the third is a multiplier, and the fourth is tolerance (e.g. gold = ±5%).
3-band resistors (no tolerance band) are implicitly ±20%; 5-band precision resistors use three significant-digit bands for ±1% or ±2% tolerance.
Resistors in series add directly (R_total = R1 + R2 + …); resistors in parallel combine as 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + …, so the parallel total is always less than the smallest single resistor.

pitfalls: A higher wattage rating means the resistor can dissipate more heat, not that it has a different resistance value — resistance and power rating are independent specs.

claims.text: A capacitor stores energy in an electric field between two conductive plates separated by an insulator; charging/discharging is never instantaneous.

The RC time constant τ = R × C is the time to charge to about 63.2% of the source voltage (or discharge to about 36.8% of the initial voltage); after 5τ a capacitor is considered fully charged/discharged (~99.3%).
Charging voltage over time follows V(t) = V_s(1 − e^(−t/RC)); discharging follows V(t) = V0 · e^(−t/RC).

pitfalls: A capacitor blocks steady DC once fully charged (no current flows through the dielectric) but passes AC — that frequency-dependent behavior is exactly what makes it useful as a filter.

claims.text: By Faraday's law, the voltage across an inductor is V_L = L(di/dt) — proportional to how fast the current through it is changing, not to the current itself.
An inductor stores energy in its magnetic field and opposes changes in current, the mirror image of a capacitor opposing changes in voltage.

An LC circuit (inductor + capacitor) oscillates at a natural resonant frequency f = 1 / (2π√(LC)), with energy sloshing back and forth between the capacitor's electric field and the inductor's magnetic field.

pitfalls: Beginners expect an inductor to behave like a plain wire; it does for steady DC, but it fights any sudden change in current, which is what causes the voltage spark when you unplug an inductive load.

claims.text: A PN junction diode is p-type semiconductor fused to n-type; where they meet, charge diffuses and leaves a depletion region with a built-in electric field that acts as a potential barrier.

Forward bias (positive terminal to the p-side) collapses the depletion region once the applied voltage exceeds the forward voltage, letting current flow easily; reverse bias widens it and blocks conduction.
Typical forward voltage drop is about 0.7 V for silicon diodes and about 0.3 V for germanium diodes.

pitfalls: A diode isn't a perfect one-way valve — it still passes a tiny reverse leakage current, and past a device-specific reverse breakdown voltage it conducts hard in reverse too (which some diodes, like Zeners, are built to exploit).

claims.text: In 1906 Lee de Forest added a third electrode, the grid, between the cathode and plate of a vacuum diode, calling the result the Audion — the first widely used device that could amplify.
A triode has three electrodes: a heated cathode (electron source), a control grid, and a plate/anode; a small voltage on the grid controls a much larger current flowing from cathode to plate.
The triode made live radio broadcasting practical and remained the core amplifying device in radio, telephone, and early computers until the transistor replaced it after 1947.

pitfalls: The grid doesn't supply the plate current — it only throttles the electron stream the heated cathode already emits, so a tiny grid-voltage change can swing a much larger plate current (that ratio is the tube's gain).

claims.text: John Bardeen and Walter Brattain demonstrated the first working point-contact transistor at Bell Labs on 16 December 1947; William Shockley's improved bipolar junction design followed in 1948 and reached mass production in the early 1950s.

An NPN bipolar transistor is a thin, lightly doped p-type base sandwiched between a heavily doped n-type emitter and an n-type collector; a small current into the base controls a much larger emitter-to-collector current.

In active mode (base-emitter forward biased, base-collector reverse biased) the transistor amplifies; in saturation (both junctions forward biased) it acts as a closed switch; in cutoff (both reverse biased) it acts as an open switch.

pitfalls: A transistor amplifies current, not power out of nowhere — the extra power in the larger collector current is drawn from the transistor's own DC supply; the base signal only controls the gate, it doesn't provide the energy.

claims.text: A radio receiver's front end is a tuned LC circuit (an antenna coil plus a variable capacitor) that resonates at the frequency f = 1/(2π√(LC)) of the desired station and rejects the rest.
A simple AM detector rectifies the tuned RF signal with a diode and smooths it with a small RC filter, recovering the audio envelope that was riding on the radio-frequency carrier.

Edwin Armstrong's superheterodyne design (patented during WWI) mixes the incoming signal with a local oscillator to produce a fixed intermediate frequency (commonly ~455 kHz) where most amplification and filtering happen, before final detection.

pitfalls: A crystal-radio-style diode detector needs no external power and can be surprisingly loud on a strong local station, but a superheterodyne's extra local-oscillator + mixer stage is what gives real sensitivity and selectivity for weak, distant, or closely-spaced stations.

volatile: Historical dates/attributions are stable; component price/availability and modern IC equivalents are not covered here and would need a refresh if added.
